## Overview

When I started looking for a dataset to anchor this first marine science project,
I had a simple question: *what data actually gets used by the people trying to
protect coral reefs in the real world?* That question led me directly to NOAA's
Coral Reef Watch (CRW) program and their Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) product —
and then to the paper that describes exactly how it's built: Skirving et al.
(2020), *CoralTemp and the Coral Reef Watch Coral Bleaching Heat Stress Product
Suite Version 3.1* [@skirving2020].

Reading that paper changed how I thought about this project.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Data source:** NOAA Coral Reef Watch Version 3.1 Daily 5km Satellite Coral
Bleaching Heat Stress Product Suite. Updated daily.
[coralreefwatch.noaa.gov](https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/)
:::

### Why not just use raw sea surface temperature?

My first instinct was to pull raw SST data — it's widely available from multiple
sources including NOAA OISST, NASA MODIS, and the Copernicus Marine Service. But
raw SST alone doesn't tell you whether a coral is stressed. A reading of 29°C
means something very different on the Great Barrier Reef versus Bonaire. What
matters isn't the absolute temperature — it's how that temperature compares to
what the corals at that location have adapted to over their lifetimes.

That realization pushed me toward NOAA CRW's derived product suite, where that
comparison is already built in — and rigorously validated against decades of field
bleaching observations worldwide.

### The climatological baseline — and why it matters so much

The entire CRW product suite rests on a climatological baseline: a set of
long-term average SST values for every location on Earth, for every month of the
year. This is not a trivial thing to build correctly. As Skirving et al. (2020)
describe, CRW spent years grappling with a fundamental problem — the satellite SST
data used to *build* the climatology came from different sensors and processing
pipelines than the near-real-time data used to *calculate* the anomalies. That
mismatch introduced systematic biases, particularly at the high temperatures most
relevant to bleaching.

The development of **CoralTemp** — a single, continuous daily SST record spanning
1985 to the present, built by carefully blending three different satellite products
— was specifically designed to solve this problem. For the first time, CRW could
compute the climatology and the daily anomalies from the *same* underlying dataset.
The Monthly Mean (MM) climatology was derived from this record across 1985–2012,
and the **Maximum Monthly Mean (MMM)** — the warmest month at each location —
became the critical threshold from which the HotSpot and DHW products are derived.

I want to be transparent about why this matters for this project: I am not
independently validating the baseline. I am trusting that NOAA CRW's 25-year
investment in getting this right is sound — which is supported by the peer-reviewed
literature and by the fact that these products are the global standard used by reef
managers worldwide. But understanding *that the baseline exists, how it was
constructed, and what its assumptions are* is essential context for interpreting
everything downstream. The DHW values we analyze are only as meaningful as the MMM
they're derived from.

### The 5km pixel limitation

One more thing worth acknowledging upfront: the Virtual Station data used in this
analysis represents a single 5×5 km satellite pixel per reef location. Real reef
systems are far more heterogeneous than that. Temperatures vary with depth, local
current patterns, reef orientation, and bathymetry — a coral colony in a deep
channel experiences very different conditions than one on a shallow exposed crest,
even within the same reef system.

This means the DHW values we analyze are best understood as a **reef-wide surface
thermal stress index** — a strong and validated predictor of bleaching risk at the
population level, but not a precise measurement of conditions at any specific
location. This is the same limitation the entire global reef monitoring community
works with, and it doesn't undermine the analysis. It just means we should be
careful about over-interpreting site-level differences that might reflect local
thermal refugia or exposure patterns rather than true differences in bleaching
pressure.

With those caveats clearly on the table, here is what this project actually does.

---

## Why This Work Matters to Me

The spikes in the DHW charts are not abstract. Each one represents a mass bleaching
event — weeks of accumulated heat stress that caused corals to expel the symbiotic
algae that give them both their color and their ability to survive. Some recovered.
Many did not.

The 2023 spike on the Florida Keys chart stopped me the first time I saw it. That
summer, sea surface temperatures off the Keys reached levels never recorded in the
satellite era — DHW values pushing well past the Alert Level 2 threshold where
mortality becomes likely. I did not feel unsettled. I felt implicated. Like I was
watching something irreplaceable disappear and I was not doing my share to stop it.

We named this planet wrong. It is not Earth. It is Ocean. The ocean covers 71% of
the surface, produces more than half the oxygen we breathe, and absorbs the heat
that our atmosphere can no longer contain. Coral reefs are less than 1% of the
ocean floor and support roughly 25% of all marine species. They are
disproportionately vital and disproportionately vulnerable — and they are bleaching
at a pace that the satellite record has never seen before.

That feeling of personal responsibility is what brought me here.

I have spent my career in biomedical and clinical research informatics — building
data pipelines, managing research data governance, working with complex health
datasets in high-stakes environments. The technical skills transfer. What I am
building now is the domain knowledge and the credibility to point those skills at
a different set of urgent problems. The ocean is not separate from human health.
It is the precondition for it.

The monitoring infrastructure that NOAA CRW has built — the same data we are
working with in this notebook — exists because early warning matters. A reef
manager who knows a bleaching event is coming two weeks in advance can act. That
connection between data, prediction, and real-world response is something I
understand from clinical informatics. I want to bring it here. I want my work to
matter outside the walls of a hospital system.

This project is a first step. The code is simple, the dataset is publicly
available, and the science belongs to the researchers at NOAA CRW and the marine
biologists doing fieldwork on these reefs. But the ability to work with this data
rigorously, document it honestly, and communicate it clearly — that is something
I can contribute. And I intend to keep going.

---

## Study Sites

Five reef systems spanning two ocean basins were selected to compare thermal
stress patterns across different geographic and ecological contexts:

| Site | NOAA CRW Station | Ocean | Notes |
|------|-----------------|-------|-------|
| Florida Keys, USA | `florida_keys` | Caribbean/Atlantic | Record-breaking bleaching 2023 |
| Cayman Islands | `cayman_islands` | Caribbean | Covers all three Cayman Islands |
| Honduras (Bay Islands) | `honduras` | Caribbean | Covers Bay Islands including Roatán |
| ABC Islands (Bonaire) | `abc_islands` | Caribbean | Aruba, Curaçao, and Bonaire |
| GBR Central | `gbr_central` | Indo-Pacific | Central sector; 2016 and 2022 mass bleaching |

These sites were chosen to represent geographic diversity across two ocean basins,
a range of documented bleaching histories, and contrasting thermal stress profiles.
Station IDs were verified directly against the live NOAA CRW Virtual Station list
at [coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data.php](https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data.php)
before any data was fetched.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**A note on the GBR:** Unlike the Caribbean sites, the Great Barrier Reef has no
single regional station — it is simply too large. NOAA splits it into five sectors:
Torres Strait, Far Northern, Northern, Central, and Southern. We selected GBR
Central, which covers the Whitsundays and Townsville region. My awareness of the
2016 bleaching event in this sector came initially from watching Jeff Orlowski's
documentary *Chasing Coral* — and I later found that the same event was the subject
of Hughes et al. (2017) in *Nature*. The documentary and the peer-reviewed
literature were pointing at the same place.
:::

---

## Science Background

### Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly

Every ocean location has a long-term average temperature for each time of year,
derived from decades of satellite records. The **SST anomaly** measures the
departure of today's temperature from that historical average:

$$\text{SST Anomaly} = \text{SST} - \text{Daily SST Climatology}$$

A positive anomaly means the ocean is warmer than usual for that season. This
metric provides broad context for ocean warming but is not on its own a reliable
predictor of coral bleaching, because corals in different locations are adapted to
different baseline temperatures.

### Coral Bleaching HotSpot

The **HotSpot** product is calibrated specifically to coral biology. It measures
how far the current temperature exceeds the *warmest month* that corals at that
location have historically experienced — a threshold called the Maximum Monthly
Mean (MMM):

$$\text{HotSpot} = \text{SST} - \text{MMM SST Climatology}, \quad \text{HotSpot} \geq 0$$

A HotSpot ≥ 1°C indicates active thermal stress. The MMM baseline varies by
location, so this metric captures *relative* stress rather than absolute
temperature — making it comparable across sites with very different ambient ocean
temperatures [@skirving2020].

### Degree Heating Weeks (DHW)

A single warm day can stress corals, but corals are resilient to brief exposures.
What causes bleaching is *sustained* thermal stress accumulating over weeks.
**Degree Heating Weeks** integrate the HotSpot values (when ≥ 1°C) over a moving
12-week window:

$$\text{DHW} = \frac{\sum_{d=0}^{83} \max(\text{HotSpot}_d - 0, 0) \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{HotSpot}_d \geq 1]}{7}$$

Units are **°C-weeks**. Think of DHW as a "heat debt" that builds up as hot
conditions persist — analogous to the accumulated dose concept in clinical
pharmacology or radiation biology.

Two key thresholds, established through satellite monitoring and field validation
[@liu2013]:

| DHW Value | Alert Level | Biological Expectation |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| < 4 °C-weeks | Watch / Warning | Thermal stress present; monitoring needed |
| ≥ 4 °C-weeks | **Alert Level 1** | Bleaching likely |
| ≥ 8 °C-weeks | **Alert Level 2** | Severe bleaching; significant mortality likely |

---

## Data & Methods

### Data Acquisition

Data were fetched programmatically from the NOAA CRW Virtual Station feed using
verified station IDs. The fetch URL pattern is:

```
https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data/{station_id}.txt
```

Each file opens with an unstructured metadata header containing station-level
information — including the **Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean (MMM)** temperature,
which we extract programmatically and carry through the analysis as the
location-specific bleaching threshold baseline.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**A note on the file format:** The NOAA CRW Virtual Station files are not simple
CSVs. The header uses plain text labels rather than `#` comment markers, the date
is split across three columns (`YYYY`, `MM`, `DD`), and the column names reflect
the updated v3.1 product suite. We inspected the raw file before writing the parser
rather than assuming a format — a step worth documenting for anyone reproducing
this analysis.
:::

Data are cached locally after first retrieval. To force a fresh download, set
`force_refresh=True` in the fetch call.

### Analysis Pipeline

1. **Fetch** — live HTTP request to CRW Virtual Station feed per site
2. **Cache** — store raw `.txt` files in `data/raw/`
3. **Parse** — extract MMM from header, build date column, rename columns
4. **Visualize** — DHW time series, alert level distributions, annual peak heatmap
5. **Interpret** — narrative analysis of key bleaching events

---

## Results

::: {#setup .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="Setup: imports, directories, thresholds"}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import requests
import os
from io import StringIO

# Paths relative to projects/ directory where this .qmd lives
CACHE_DIR = "data/raw"
FIG_DIR   = "figures"
os.makedirs(CACHE_DIR, exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(FIG_DIR,   exist_ok=True)

# Bleaching thresholds (Skirving et al. 2020, Liu et al. 2013)
DHW_WARNING = 4   # Alert Level 1: Bleaching Likely
DHW_SEVERE  = 8   # Alert Level 2: Mortality Likely
```
:::


::: {#sites .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="Study site definitions and verified NOAA CRW station IDs"}

    },

    },
```

```
✓ Florida Keys                   | station: florida_keys
✓ Cayman Islands                 | station: cayman_islands
✓ Honduras (Bay Islands)         | station: honduras
✓ ABC Islands (Bonaire)          | station: abc_islands
✓ GBR Central                    | station: gbr_central
```

::: {#fetch .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="Fetch and parse NOAA CRW Virtual Station data"}
CRW_BASE_URL = "https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data/{station_id}.txt"

def parse_crw_header(lines):
    """Extract metadata from the CRW file header block."""
    meta = {}
    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
        line_stripped = line.strip()
        if line_stripped == "Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean:":
            meta["mmm"] = float(lines[i + 1].strip())
        if line_stripped == "Name:":
            meta["name"] = lines[i + 1].strip()
        if line_stripped.startswith("YYYY MM DD"):
            meta["data_start_line"] = i
            break
    return meta

def fetch_crw_station(site_name, station_id, force_refresh=False):
    cache_path = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, f"{station_id}.txt")

    if not force_refresh and os.path.exists(cache_path):
        print(f"  ✓ Loaded from cache: {site_name}")
        with open(cache_path, "r") as f:
            raw_lines = f.readlines()
    else:
        url = CRW_BASE_URL.format(station_id=station_id)
        print(f"  ↓ Fetching live: {site_name}")
        resp = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        raw_lines = resp.text.splitlines(keepends=True)
        with open(cache_path, "w") as f:
            f.writelines(raw_lines)

    meta = parse_crw_header(raw_lines)
    data_start = meta["data_start_line"]

    data_text = "".join(raw_lines[data_start:])
    df = pd.read_csv(
        StringIO(data_text),
        sep=r"\s+",
        na_values=["-999", "-9999", "-99.0000"],
    )

    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
        df["YYYY"].astype(str) + "-" +
        df["MM"].astype(str).str.zfill(2) + "-" +
        df["DD"].astype(str).str.zfill(2)
    )

    df.rename(columns={
        "SST@90th_HS":        "sst",
        "SSTA@90th_HS":       "sst_anomaly",
        "90th_HS>0":          "hotspot",
        "DHW_from_90th_HS>1": "dhw",
        "BAA_7day_max":       "alert",
    }, inplace=True)

    df["site"] = site_name
    df["mmm"]  = meta.get("mmm", None)

    keep = ["date", "sst", "sst_anomaly", "hotspot", "dhw", "alert", "site", "mmm"]
    df = df[[c for c in keep if c in df.columns]]
    df.sort_values("date", inplace=True)
    df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

    print(f"    ✓ {len(df):,} rows | "
          f"{df['date'].min().date()} → {df['date'].max().date()} | "
          f"MMM: {meta.get('mmm')}°C")
    return df


print("Fetching NOAA CRW Virtual Station data...\n")
all_data = {}
for site_name, cfg in SITES.items():
    df = fetch_crw_station(site_name, cfg["station_id"])
    all_data[site_name] = df

print(f"\n✓ Done. {len(all_data)} sites loaded.")
```

```
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 29.6264°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: Cayman Islands
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 29.3925°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: Honduras (Bay Islands)
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 28.87°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: ABC Islands (Bonaire)
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 28.0193°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: GBR Central
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 28.3422°C

✓ Done. 5 sites loaded.
```
:::
:::


### MMM Baseline Values

The MMM values extracted from the file headers reveal the location-specific
thermal baselines — before a single chart is plotted:

::: {#mmm-table .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="MMM baseline values per site"}
mmm_rows = [
    {"Site": name, "MMM (°C)": df["mmm"].iloc[0]}
    for name, df in all_data.items()
]
mmm_df = pd.DataFrame(mmm_rows).sort_values("MMM (°C)", ascending=False)
mmm_df["MMM (°C)"] = mmm_df["MMM (°C)"].map("{:.2f}".format)
print(mmm_df.to_string(index=False))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
                  Site MMM (°C)
          Florida Keys    29.63
        Cayman Islands    29.39
Honduras (Bay Islands)    28.87
           GBR Central    28.34
 ABC Islands (Bonaire)    28.02
```
:::
:::


This is the MMM doing exactly what Skirving et al. (2020) described — capturing
location-specific thermal tolerance rather than absolute temperature. A raw SST
reading of 29°C represents no stress at all in the Florida Keys, but would already
be pushing past the bleaching threshold on the GBR.

### DHW Time Series

The figure below shows Degree Heating Week time series for all five reef sites
from 2000 to present. Horizontal dashed lines mark the Alert Level 1 (4 °C-weeks)
and Alert Level 2 (8 °C-weeks) thresholds. Each panel also shows the site's MMM
baseline temperature.

::: {#cell-fig-timeseries .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="DHW time series — all five sites"}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(
    nrows=len(all_data), ncols=1,
    figsize=(14, 3.2 * len(all_data)),
    sharex=False
)

colors = ["#0077b6", "#00b4d8", "#48cae4", "#90e0ef", "#e07a5f"]

for ax, (site, df), color in zip(axes, all_data.items(), colors):
    mask = df["date"] >= "2000-01-01"
    plot_df = df[mask].copy()

    ax.fill_between(plot_df["date"], plot_df["dhw"],
                    alpha=0.25, color=color)
    ax.plot(plot_df["date"], plot_df["dhw"],
            color=color, lw=1.0, label="DHW")

    ax.axhline(DHW_WARNING, color="#f4a261", ls="--", lw=1.2,
               label=f"Alert L1 ({DHW_WARNING} °C-wks)")
    ax.axhline(DHW_SEVERE, color="#e63946", ls="--", lw=1.2,
               label=f"Alert L2 ({DHW_SEVERE} °C-wks)")

    ax.set_title(
        f"{site}  |  MMM: {df['mmm'].iloc[0]:.2f}°C",
        fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", loc="left"
    )
    ax.set_ylabel("DHW (°C-weeks)", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylim(bottom=0)
    ax.legend(fontsize=7, loc="upper left", framealpha=0.7)
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25)
    ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

axes[-1].set_xlabel("Date", fontsize=10)
fig.suptitle(
    "NOAA CRW Degree Heating Weeks by Reef Site (2000–present)\n"
    "Data: NOAA Coral Reef Watch Version 3.1",
    fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(FIG_DIR, "dhw_timeseries_all_sites.png"),
            dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![DHW time series for all five reef sites (2000–present). Dashed lines mark bleaching alert thresholds. MMM values shown in panel titles. Data: NOAA CRW Version 3.1.](bleaching-risk-notebook_files/figure-html/fig-timeseries-output-1.png){#fig-timeseries width=1333 height=1556}
:::
:::


Key observations:

- **Florida Keys** — the 2023 spike dwarfs everything before it in the satellite
  record, consistent with the unprecedented marine heat wave that summer.
- **GBR Central** — the 2016 and 2022 events are clearly visible; the lower MMM
  baseline means bleaching stress kicks in at a lower absolute temperature.
- **ABC Islands (Bonaire)** — spikes are consistently lower than the other
  Caribbean sites, consistent with its reputation for relatively robust reef health.
- **All Caribbean sites** — show a clear pattern of increasing frequency and
  magnitude of DHW exceedances from 2015 onward.

---

## Interpretation & Limitations

### What this analysis shows

This analysis demonstrates the utility of satellite thermal monitoring for
tracking bleaching risk across geographically distributed reef systems. The DHW
metric — developed and validated by NOAA CRW scientists over more than 20 years —
provides a biologically grounded, globally consistent index of accumulated thermal
stress that has been correlated with field observations of bleaching onset and
severity.

### Limitations to note

- **Point data:** CRW Virtual Station data represent a single 5×5 km pixel per
  site. Actual reef conditions vary at finer spatial scales due to bathymetry,
  currents, and local upwelling.
- **No in situ validation:** This analysis does not include field bleaching surveys
  or coral cover data. DHW is a *risk indicator*, not a direct measurement of
  bleaching.
- **Alert thresholds are global:** The 4 and 8 °C-week thresholds are population-
  level averages. Some coral communities may have higher or lower thermal tolerance.
- **GBR representation:** The `gbr_central` station represents one of five GBR
  sectors. Thermal stress patterns vary considerably across the reef's 2,300 km
  length.

---

## Future Directions

This MVP analysis establishes the data pipeline and visualization framework.
Planned extensions include:

- **All five GBR sectors** — compare Torres Strait, Far Northern, Northern,
  Central, and Southern to examine latitudinal bleaching gradients
- **Spatial raster analysis** — process global CRW NetCDF files via ERDDAP to
  produce regional DHW maps
- **Bleaching observation integration** — overlay NOAA CoRIS or Reef Check field
  survey data to validate satellite signals against in-water observations
- **ENSO correlation** — correlate DHW peaks with Niño 3.4 SST anomaly index
- **Trend analysis** — fit linear trend models to annual peak DHW to quantify
  warming trajectories at each site

---

## About This Project

This project is part of a portfolio in marine environmental data science,
developed to demonstrate reproducible analysis workflows applied to real-world
reef conservation data. The full code, data pipeline, and environment
specification are available on GitHub.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Reproducibility
This page renders live from embedded Python — data is fetched from NOAA CRW,
parsed, and plotted on every render unless frozen. Outputs are frozen and
committed to the repository via `freeze: true`. To re-execute from scratch,
delete `_freeze/projects/bleaching-risk-notebook/` from the portfolio site repo
and re-render. The `uv` environment is fully specified in the
[coral-bleaching-dhw](https://github.com/velezf/coral-bleaching-dhw)
repository's `requirements.txt`.
:::

[View source repo](https://github.com/velezf/coral-bleaching-dhw){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[NOAA Coral Reef Watch](https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/){.btn .btn-outline-secondary}
